title: The Anger of Surviving What You Weren't Meant To
dek: You survived the unsurvivable, and now your body is a furnace. This is not a malfunction; it is the biological cost of staying alive when the world demanded your extinction, and your fury is the only thing keeping the cold out.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Anger of Surviving What You Weren't Meant To


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.178Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You don’t have to look far to feel the weight of your survival anger. It’s lodged deep in the masseter muscle, that hard, clenched jawline that tightens every time you remember what you were told to swallow whole, what you were meant to keep silent. The teeth press together with the persistence of a night that refuses to yield, the body’s first line of defense when words fail. You know this ache, the subtle burn just beneath the skin, that thick, unyielding tension that sits like a stone in your face, stubborn and unyielding. It’s not just in your jaw; it’s a visceral, full-body experience that surges through your fascia and muscle, a reminder that you are still here, still fighting.

The psoas muscle, that deep core stabilizer wrapping around your lumbar vertebrae, holds a story of survival too. It tightens in a way that’s almost anticipatory, as if bracing against an unseen blow. You might notice it one early, grey morning when the damp chill settles into your lower back, or in the middle of a restless night when your breath catches shallow in your diaphragm. This is not pathology, this is biology - your body’s honest accounting of the storms you’ve weathered. The psoas remembers the moments you were told you shouldn’t have made it out, that your life was a fragile thread. And still, here you are, muscles hardwired in the tension of persistence.

The trapezius and levator scapulae muscles trap the rage in a physical chokehold. These muscles, spanning the neck and the upper shoulders, knit themselves into tight knots by late afternoon when the day’s burdens have piled high, when the air is thick with humidity or the electric charge of an impending storm. You can feel the tension in the base of your skull, sharp and unrelenting, radiating down the spine as if your nervous system is holding a silent scream. It’s the body’s way of saying, Enough. You survived. Now, what do you do with this rage that pulses like a low drumbeat beneath your skin?

Anger is often framed as ugly, something to be tamed or tucked away like a wild animal. But the body does not lie. The jaw remembers every time you bit down on words you weren’t allowed to say, the psoas locks in the memory of every moment you braced against collapse, the trapezius and levator scapulae bear the weight of carrying what should have crushed you. This is not fury as pathology; it is the raw, unfiltered biology of survival. It is the ache of living beyond the margins set for you, the pulse of a life that was never meant to be sustained but insists on existing anyway.

You might find this anger rising in the quiet half-light before dawn, when the world is still and your body is the loudest voice in the room. It smells like sweat and earth, the musk of your own resilience. It’s not a polite feeling wrapped in soft words; it is the fierce, relentless heat of survival etched into each muscle and tendon. The body knows this anger intimately because it has carried it through every step, every breath, every heartbeat that refused to be extinguished.

In this unvarnished moment, your anger is not an enemy but a testament. It holds the truth of your story, the unspoken chapters of enduring and outlasting. This is the anger of surviving what you weren’t meant to, and it is as much a part of your body as the blood running through your veins and the slow, steady thrum of your heart. To honor this anger is to honor your body’s unyielding will to live.

## The Body Keeps the Score - Of Fury and Survival

You carry the memory of your fury not just in your mind but deep in the fibers of your body. This is not metaphor or poetic license; this is biology, unvarnished and unyielding. Bessel van der Kolk’s work on trauma teaches us that the body remembers what the mind cannot always name. When you were taught your anger was ugly, something vital was stifled, but your body did not forget. The masseter muscles along your jawline, the trapezius climbing your neck and shoulders, even the subtle tension beneath your diaphragm - all catalog this defiance and this survival in the language of muscle and fascia.

Think of the levator scapulae, those small muscles that lift your shoulder blades. When threat arrives, whether it was a shouted word in childhood or a cold dismissal in adulthood, these muscles tense in preparation for fight or flight. They brace you against the world’s assault. You may not register this tension consciously, but it is there, pressing into your body like a silent scream. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body is primed to protect you, to keep you alive. The jaw clenches, the shoulders hunch, the breath shortens. This is your body’s way of bearing witness to what you endured.

You might notice, in the quiet hours just before dawn, the weight of this stored memory settling unevenly across your body. The psoas muscles, those deep, central stabilizers nestled beside your lumbar spine, often carry the burden of chronic threat. When you lie in bed, the air cool against your skin, the psoas tightens - an unconscious readiness to spring into action, to defend, to resist. You may smell the faint musk of your own sweat, mingled with the herbal teas you brewed to soothe a restless night, but still, the body remembers. It remembers every time you swallowed your rage, every time you forced your shoulders down, telling yourself to “be nice,” “be quiet,” “be small.” 

Van der Kolk says the body keeps the score, but here, I want to emphasize: it keeps the score of your fury, too. Your anger is not a disease or a flaw; it is a fierce, elemental force etched into the fascia that wraps your muscles like vines. It is there in the tight bands of tissue around your diaphragm that constrict your breath when you try to speak your truth but are met with resistance. It is in the subtle twitch of your hands, the clenching of your fists, the grinding of your teeth as you navigate a world that told you to hide that fire. Your body is not a passive vessel; it is an archive, a ledger of survival and resistance.

The somatic experience of anger is often shamed, but it is as ancient and necessary as the muscles that move your spine. Your diaphragm, the great dome beneath your lungs, contracts differently when you are angry - short, sharp breaths that demand release. This is your body’s way of mobilizing energy, gathering strength to respond, not to collapse. You don’t have to apologize for this. The tightness in your trapezius and levator scapulae is not a weakness; it is a testament to your endurance. The body remembers what your mind might want to forget: that anger was your armor, your signal, your power.

In the quiet moments, when the world is still and you can feel the subtle hum of tension in your neck and shoulders, recognize that this is your body holding space for your fury. It is telling you a story of survival, not sickness. When you touch the tender spot in your masseter or notice the ache along your spine, you are encountering the living record of your refusal to be erased or silenced. This is the biology of survival - raw, unapologetic, and embodied. Your body knows, the jaw remembers, and until you do, this story will continue to be written in muscle and breath.

## The Quiet Work of Herbs on Your Nervous System

You carry more than memories in your body; you carry the imprints of survival, the tension in the masseter that clenches when you thought you had to be invisible, the coil of your psoas tightening at the faintest echo of danger. This is not pathology, this is biology speaking in muscle and fascia, in the tightness behind your eyes, the shallow rise of your diaphragm when the world feels too sharp. The herbs I reach for are not magic wands but allies with deep, slow medicine, each one a whisper to your nervous system telling it, “You can rest now.”

Take milky oats (Avena sativa), for instance. It’s not a flashy herb, but in the late-night hours when your trapezius flares up, knotted like a noose around your neck, milky oats hums a steady lullaby to your nervous system. It nourishes the nervous tissue itself, like a warm balm in the fascia that wraps your spinal erectors, soothing the frayed edges of your resilience. Herbalist Susun Weed often describes milky oats as a restorative tonic, and it is exactly that - fortifying without overstimulating, encouraging the vagus nerve to release its gentle parasympathetic embrace. When you sip milky oats tincture or steep its green seed heads in a slow, thoughtful tea, you’re inviting your nervous system to remember what calm feels like beneath the layers of learned fury and survival tension.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) is another herb that knows the language of your nervous system intimately. The masseter muscle, that jaw muscle so likely to clench at the slightest provocation, remembers the sharp edges of anger you were taught to swallow. Skullcap doesn’t just settle the mind; it reaches into the subtle currents of your nervous system, particularly the sympathetic branch that’s been revved too high for too long. Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) identifies this as a heat pattern in the Liver channel, where anger and frustration get stuck like a storm cloud refusing to pass. Skullcap cools this heat, softening the levator scapulae and trapezius muscles that tense with every unresolved “shouldn’t.” It’s a nervine sedative, a quiet companion when the night is too loud with thoughts and the body aches with unspent fury. When you prepare skullcap as a tea or tincture, you’re not just calming nerves; you’re speaking directly to the fascia that remembers how to hold tension and asking it to let go.

Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) is another herb you’ll want in your arsenal, especially when the weight of survival sits heavy on your chest, the diaphragm constricted like a fist around your breath. This is not just about anxiety; this is about the heart-mind connection, the shen as TCM calls it, which motherwort gently soothes. It’s for the nights when your breath is short, your chest tight as if the world is closing in, and the trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles scream in silent protest. Motherwort opens this constriction, inviting the diaphragm to expand fully again, to press down and massage the organs below, restoring the natural rhythm of breath and rest. Herbalists like Matthew Wood highlight motherwort’s ability to clear stagnation in the chest and calm the nervous system without dulling your edge. This is crucial for you, whose fury was never meant to be buried but directed and honored.

These herbs don’t act in isolation. They weave together in your body like threads in the fascia, each supporting the other. When you combine milky oats, skullcap, and motherwort, you’re crafting a medicine for the nervous system that respects its complexity - the way your jaw remembers clenched rage, your diaphragm remembers breath caught in fear, your trapezius remembers the weight of carrying more than you should have to. This is not about erasing your fury but about tending to the body that holds it, so it can transform from burden to power.

If you’re curious to explore these allies further, you might start with something like [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20), a carefully curated blend that honors the nervous system’s need for both strength and ease. But remember, this work is as much about listening to your body’s unique story as it is about the herbs themselves. In the quiet hours before dawn, when the world feels too much and your body is both fortress and battlefield, these plants stand with you - patient, unshaken, ready to help your nervous system remember how to come home.

## The Quiet Weight Beneath Fury: Grief and the Inherited Body

When you sit with your anger, especially the kind that feels ancient, it’s not just yours. Resmaa Menakem, in his work on somatic abolitionism, teaches us that trauma is a body memory passed down through generations - it’s not only in our stories but in our muscles, our fascia, the very architecture of our nervous system. Your anger, the one you were taught to hide or feel ashamed for, is not just a personal flame flickering in isolation. It’s the echo of survival etched into the masseter muscles that clench when you’re startled awake at 3 a.m., or the tight coil in your psoas that curls like a fist inside your belly when you brace against the world. This is not pathology, this is biology.

You might notice how the levator scapulae - the muscles that lift your shoulders toward your ears - tense up without permission when you encounter certain people or situations. That tension is old grief, layered beneath your fury. It’s the grief of those who came before you, whose pain never found words or ceremonies, only clenched jaws and held breath. Menakem calls it “post-traumatic slavery syndrome” for a reason: the body remembers even when the mind refuses to speak. Anger is the body’s attempt to protect, to survive, but underneath it lives a quieter, heavier companion - grief.

Grief isn’t always the sobbing kind you expect. Sometimes it’s the slow, dull ache in the diaphragm, the muscle that contracts beneath your ribcage every time you swallow a bitter memory or swallow your voice. When you’re taught your fury is ugly, you learn to hold that grief tight, too, because expressing it might unravel the careful containment built by necessity. You feel it as a knot in your throat or a hollow behind your sternum, a place where ancestors’ tears pooled in secret. This grief is not a sign of weakness but a sign of deep survival - an inheritance you carry in your body’s connective tissue, waiting to be acknowledged.

As the night deepens and the sky cools to a damp, blue-black, you might find your body responding to old grief in subtle ways. The trapezius muscles, spanning from your neck to your mid-back, might twitch or burn, a reminder of all the times you had to be ‘strong’ for others while hiding your own pain. This is where somatic abolitionism becomes an act of radical self-care: by tuning into these sensations, you honor the lineage of survival encoded in your own tissues. You begin to understand that your anger, raw and unfiltered, is a response to grief that has no other outlet.

I remember sitting with Ruth King, who taught me that this grief is a doorway - not to despair, but to deeper healing. She showed me how the body knows, how it carries what the mind cannot bear to hold. When you allow your diaphragm to release, when you let your jaw unclench and the masseter muscles soften, you give your body permission to grieve. This is an act of courage. It’s not about erasing the anger or pretending it doesn’t exist. Instead, it’s about acknowledging the full spectrum of your survival - your rage and your sorrow intertwined, each demanding recognition.

Herbs can support this process, but they are never a quick fix or a way to numb the body’s wisdom. They are serious medicine that respects the complexity of your experience. For example, a carefully prepared tincture of skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) might help soothe the nervous system, inviting the diaphragm to soften and the breath to deepen. Meanwhile, a tea made from hawthorn berries can gently support the heart’s capacity to hold grief without collapsing. These plant allies work with your body, not against it, helping you navigate the heavy waters of inherited sorrow while honoring the fierce anger that kept you alive.

## A Somatic Practice for the Clenched Jaw

When the world demands you swallow your fury, the masseter muscle is often the first to take the hit. It clenches, it grinds, it holds the unspoken words like a vault. This practice is not about forcing the jaw to relax - that would just be another demand on a body that’s already working too hard. Instead, it’s about inviting curiosity into the tension, letting the body know it’s safe to soften, even just a fraction.

Find a quiet place where you won’t be interrupted. You can sit or lie down, whichever feels more supportive for your spine. Close your eyes if that feels comfortable, or let your gaze rest softly on a spot in front of you. Take a slow, natural breath, noticing how the air moves through your nostrils and down into your chest. Don’t try to change the breath; just observe it.

Now, bring your awareness to your jaw. Notice the hinge where the upper and lower jaw meet, just in front of your ears. Feel the density of the masseter muscle there. Is it tight? Is it humming with unspent energy? Acknowledge the tension without judgment. Say to yourself, silently or aloud, “I feel you holding on. Thank you for protecting me.”

Gently place the pads of your index and middle fingers on the masseter muscles on both sides of your face. Don’t press hard; just let the weight of your fingers rest there, like a warm blanket. Imagine the heat from your fingertips melting into the fascia, slowly warming the tight fibers. Breathe into that space. With each exhale, imagine the muscle fibers unspooling, just a millimeter at a time.

If your jaw feels ready, allow your lips to part slightly. Let the lower jaw hang heavy, as if gravity is gently pulling it down. You might feel a stretch along the sides of your face or even down into your neck. If the tension flares up, that’s okay. Just pause, breathe, and remind your body that it doesn’t have to let go all at once. The body knows its own pace.

Stay with this sensation for a few minutes. Notice if the tension shifts or moves to another part of your body, like the trapezius or the psoas. When you’re ready to close the practice, take a deep breath in, filling your belly, and exhale with a soft sigh. Slowly remove your hands and open your eyes. Remember, this is not a one-time fix; it’s a conversation with your body, a way of honoring the anger that kept you alive while inviting in the possibility of rest.

## FAQ: Honoring the Body’s Fury

**Q:** Is my anger toxic? I’ve been told I need to let it go or it will poison me.  
**A:** No. You are not toxic because you carry survival anger. The masseter muscle clenches when you’re told to “calm down” or “be nice,” and that tension is just your body’s way of holding space for feelings you were never allowed to express. This is not pathology, this is biology. Anger is a signal, a fire alarm in your nervous system, not a sign that you are inherently harmful. Think about the way your skin might flare after a long day of holding back tears and words - your body is trying to tell you something. Your fury is a form of self-defense that has kept you alive, even if it feels raw or unwieldy now. It’s not a poison inside you; it’s a medicine waiting for the right conditions to be understood and transformed. When you treat your anger as a symptom to be suppressed, the muscles around your ribs and spine tighten in protest. But when you invite curiosity, when you let the body speak through subtle fascial releases or a tincture of chamomile and valerian at dusk, you create space for healing. Toxicity is a story told by shame, not by the truth of your body’s wisdom.

**Q:** How do I know if I’m overreacting or if this anger is valid?  
**A:** The body doesn’t lie, even if your mind tries to rewrite the story. Notice where the anger lives: is it a hot pulse behind your eyes, a grinding in your temporalis muscle, or a dull throb in your lower back? These sensations are clues. Overreaction is often a social judgment imposed from outside, but your body’s response is always valid. Teachers like Deb Dana remind us that the nervous system is constantly scanning for safety, and if it senses threat, the psoas will contract, the diaphragm will seize, and your heart rate will rise. This is not an overreaction; it’s a survival response calibrated by your past. Your anger is a message about boundaries crossed or needs unmet, not a personal failing. You might find relief in somatic practices that focus on releasing the tension in your masseter and trapezius muscles, or in herbal allies like blue vervain, which can help the nervous system recalibrate without shutting down your feelings. Trust the body’s wisdom more than external voices that want you to minimize your experience.

**Q:** What if I express this anger and people reject me?  
**A:** This fear is real and rooted in the way you were taught to hide and shrink. The jaw remembers every moment you bit it tight to prevent words from spilling out, every time your diaphragm tightened like a cage. Expressing anger can feel like stepping into a storm with bare skin. But survival anger is your truth insisting on being heard; it’s not a threat to your worth but a boundary setting forth your needs. The body carries the memory of rejection in the levator scapulae, where tension can feel like a vise squeezing the neck, especially on cold, damp mornings. You don’t have to shout or rage to be heard; gentle, steady somatic awareness paired with herbs like hawthorn or passionflower can help you find your voice without losing your center. The risk of rejection is real, but the greater risk is silencing yourself until your muscles and fascia harden into armor. Your anger, expressed with care and respect, is a declaration of your aliveness, not an invitation for dismissal.

**Q:** Can herbs really help with this kind of anger?  
**A:** Absolutely. Herbs are not a shortcut or a bandaid; they are medicine grown from the earth’s own fierce intelligence. When your psoas is clenched in fight or flight, when your diaphragm tightens and your breath shortens at midnight, herbs like valerian, skullcap, and motherwort can soothe the nervous system and relax the muscles that hold your fury captive. I learned this from teachers who honor the body’s wisdom, like Matthew Wood and Rosemary Gladstar, who remind us that these plants have been our allies long before modern medicine boxed emotions into disorders. Taking a well-crafted tincture or a carefully brewed decoction can feel like a conversation with the earth itself, a way to invite your body to soften its grip without diminishing your rightful rage. Herbs support the muscles and fascia as they release, helping your diaphragm to expand and your jaw to unclench, allowing your anger to be felt without overwhelming your entire system.

In the end, survival anger is a fierce medicine that you carry in your body’s very architecture - your muscles, your fascia, your breath. It is not a flaw or a burden, but a signal that you are alive and have endured. Listening to it with curiosity, respect, and care - through somatic awareness and the wise use of herbs - allows you to reclaim your power without losing your edges. The body knows its own story, and when you honor that, anger becomes not a curse but a guide.
